This is a hypothesis quite familiar in particular among people working with analytic spaces. The non-singularity hypothesis that they usually make (which means that \(X\) and \(Y\) are “smooth over \(\mathbf{C}\)”, cf. 5) seems due only to the fear that the singular points of algebraic varieties or analytic spaces still inspire in many geometers.
Let us point out the following particular case of 6:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8

Let \(X\) be an \(S\)-prescheme, \(g\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S[t_{1},\dots,t_{n}]\) an \(S\)-morphism, defined by the sections \(g_{i}\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\)) of \(\mathscr{O}_{X}\), \(x\) a point of \(X\) such that \(X\) is smooth over \(S\) at \(x\). For \(g\) to be étale at \(x\), it is necessary and sufficient that the \(d g_{i}\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\)) form a basis of \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}\) at \(x\) (or, what amounts to the same thing, that their images in \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}(x) = \big(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}\big)_{x}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{x}}\mathit{k}(x)\) form a basis of this vector space over \(\mathit{k}(x)\)).

Let \(X\) be a prescheme, \(Y\) a closed sub-prescheme of \(X\) defined by a coherent sheaf \(\mathscr{J}\) of ideals. Then \(\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2\) can be regarded as a coherent sheaf on \(Y\) (the conormal sheaf of \(Y\) in \(X\)). If now \(X\) is an \(S\)-prescheme, one has a canonical exact sequence of quasi-coherent sheaves on \(Y\)

\[ \tag{4.3} {\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2\xrightarrow{d} \mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{X}}\mathscr{O}_{Y}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathit{\Omega}^1_{Y/S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0} \]

whose right-hand part is none other than (4.2) (with the roles of \(X\) and \(Y\) interchanged, taking into account that \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{Y/X}=0\)), while the homomorphism \(\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{X}}\mathscr{O}_{Y}\) is deduced from the (in general non- linear) homomorphism \(g\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}d g\) by passage to quotients. The exactness of (4.3) is classical and moreover trivial, and is interpreted in the affine case by the following exact sequence (corresponding to a surjective homomorphism \(B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C\) of \(A\)-algebras, with kernel \(J\)):

blocks.label: 4.3\textrm  {bis}
blocks.html: \[ \tag{4.3\textrm{bis}} {J/J^2\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\Omega^1_{B/A}\otimes_{B}C\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\Omega^1_{C/A}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\qquad (C=B/J)} \]
(an exact sequence which had already been used implicitly in the proof of (I 2)!).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 9

Let \(X\) be an \(S\)-prescheme, \(Y\) a closed sub-prescheme of \(X\) defined by a coherent sheaf \(\mathscr{J}\) of ideals on \(X\), \(x\) a point of \(X\), \(g_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant n\)) sections of \(\mathscr{O}_X\), defining an \(S\)-morphism

\[ g \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S[t_1,\dots,t_n] = X' \]

finally \(p\) an integer, \(0 \leqslant p \leqslant n\). Assume \(X\) smooth over \(S\) at \(x\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    There exists an open neighbourhood \(X_1\) of \(x\) in \(X\) such that \(g|X_1\) is étale and that \(Y_1=Y \cap X_1\) (trace of \(Y\) on \(X_1\)) is the inverse image of the closed sub-prescheme \(Y'=S[t_{p+1},\dots,t_n]\) of \(X'=S[t_1,\dots,t_n]\) (i.e. the \(g_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant p\)) generate \(\mathscr{J}|X_1\)):

    \[ \xymatrix{{Y_1}\ar[d]\ar[r]&{X_1}\ar[d]^{\'etale}\\{Y'=S[t_{p+1},\dots, t_n]}\ar[r]&{X'=S[t_1,\dots,t_n]}} \]
  2. (ii)

    \(Y\) is smooth over \(S\) at \(x\), the \(g_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant p\)) define elements of \(\mathscr{J}_x\), the \(d g_i(x)\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant n\)) form a basis of \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}(x)\) over \(\mathit{k}(x)\), the \(dg'_i(x)\) (\(p+1 \leqslant i \leqslant n\)) form a basis of \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{Y/S}(x)\) over \(\mathit{k}(x)\) (where the \(g'_i\) denote the restrictions of the \(g_i\) to \(Y\); the differentials are taken with respect to \(S\)).

  3. (iii)

    The \(g_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant p\)) define a system of generators of \(\mathscr{J}_x\), and the \(dg_i(x)\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant n\)) form a basis of \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}(x)\) over \(\mathit{k}(x)\).

  4. (iv)

    \(Y\) is smooth over \(S\) at \(x\), the \(g_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant p\)) form a minimal system of generators of \(\mathscr{J}_x\), the \(dg'_i(x)\) (\(p+1 \leqslant i \leqslant n\)) form a basis of \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{Y/S}(x)\) over \(\mathit{k}(x)\).

Moreover, under these conditions, \(\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2\) is a free Module on \(Y\) at \(x\), admitting as basis at \(x\) the classes of the \(g_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant p\)), and the canonical homomorphism \(\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S} \otimes \mathscr{O}_Y\) is universally injective at \(x\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark

This implies that \(p\) is indeed determined by the other conditions, either as the rank of the free Module \(\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2\) on \(Y\) at \(x\), or again as the minimum number of generators of \(\mathscr{J}_x\) on \(X\), or finally by the fact that the relative dimension of \(Y\) rel. \(S\) at \(x\) is \(n-p\).
Proof

Suppose first that (i) holds. Then by (I 6 (iii)) \(Y_1\) is étale over \(Y'\), hence by definition it is smooth over \(S\) at \(x\) (of relative dimension \(n-p\)); the same therefore holds for \(Y\). It then follows from (8) that the \(dg_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant n\)) form a basis of \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}\) at \(x\), and that the \(dg'_i\) (\(p+1 \leqslant i \leqslant n\)) form a basis of \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{Y/S}\) at \(x\), whence it follows by the exact sequence (4.3) that the \(g_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant p\)) are linearly independent in \(\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2\) (considered as a Module over \(Y\)) at \(x\); since the \(g_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant p\)) generate \(\mathscr{J}_x\), it follows that the \(g_i \mod \mathscr{J}_x^2\) form a basis of \(\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2\) at \(x\). This implies on the one hand that the \(g_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant p\)) form a minimal system of generators of \(\mathscr{J}_x\), and on the other hand that the homomorphism \(\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S} \otimes \mathscr{O}_Y\) of (4.3) is universally injective at \(x\) (for it maps a basis of a free Module at \(x\) onto a part of a basis of a free Module at \(x\) — N.B. these are \(Y\)-Modules). This proves that (i) implies (ii), (iii), (iv), as well as the last assertions of proposition 9.
(iii) implies (i) by virtue of corollary 8.

(ii) implies (i). Indeed, the first hypothesis in (ii) means that (up to replacing \(X\) by an open neighbourhood of \(x\) in \(X\)) \(g\) induces a morphism \(h\colon Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\). According to 8, the two other hypotheses of (ii) mean that \(g\) is étale at \(x\), and \(h\) is étale at \(x\). Let then \(Y''\) be the inverse image of \(Y'\) by \(g\). Then \(Y\) is a closed sub-prescheme of \(Y''\), which is étale over \(Y'\) at \(x\) by (I 6 (iii)) since \(g\) is étale at \(x\). Hence the immersion morphism \(Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y''\) is itself étale (I 8), hence an open immersion (I 8 or I 2), so that, replacing \(X\) once more by a suitable open neighbourhood \(X_1\) of \(x\), we obtain (i).

What precedes establishes the equivalence of the conditions (i), (ii), (iii), and the fact that they imply (iv); it remains to prove that (iv)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii), which is immediate (taking into account that \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}\) is free over \(X\) at \(x\)) once one knows that the fact that \(Y\) is smooth over \(S\) at \(x\) implies that \(\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2\) is free over \(Y\) at \(x\), and the homomorphism \(\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}\otimes \mathscr{O}_Y\) is universally injective at \(x\). This last point is contained in the

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 10

Let \(X\) be a smooth \(S\)-prescheme, \(Y\) a closed sub-prescheme of \(X\) defined by a coherent sheaf \(\mathscr{J}\) of ideals on \(X\), \(x\) a point of \(X\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(Y\) is smooth over \(S\) at \(x\).

  2. (ii)

    There exist an open neighbourhood \(X_1\) of \(x\) in \(X\) and an étale \(S\)-morphism

    \[ g\colon X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'=S[t_1,\dots,t_n] \]

    such that \(Y_1=Y \cap X_1\) (the trace of \(Y\) on \(X_1\)) is the sub-prescheme of \(X_1\) that is the inverse image by \(g\) of the closed sub-prescheme \(Y'=S[t_{p+1},\dots,t_n]\) of \(X'=S[t_1,\dots,t_n]\), for a suitable \(p\).

  3. (iii)

    There exist generators \(g_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant p\)) of \(\mathscr{J}_x\), such that the \(dg_i\) form a part of a basis of \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}\) at \(x\) (or, what amounts to the same, such that the \(dg_i(x)\) in \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}\) are linearly independent over \(\mathit{k}(x)\)).

  4. (iv)

    The sheaf \(\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2\) is free over \(Y\) at \(x\), and the canonical homomorphism

    \[ d\colon \mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}\otimes \mathscr{O}_Y \]

    is universally injective at \(x\); or again: the sequence of canonical homomorphisms

    \[ 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}\otimes \mathscr{O}_Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathit{\Omega}^1_{Y/S} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

    is exact at \(x\), and \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{Y/S}\) is locally free at \(x\).
Proof

We already know that (ii) implies (i), (iii), (iv) by what precedes. Let us prove that (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) (which will at the same time complete the proof of 9). By virtue of theorem 3 (ii), the two last terms in the exact sequence (4.3) are free Modules over \(Y\). Hence, since the images in \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_X} \mathscr{O}_Y\) of the \(dg\) (\(g \in \mathscr{O}_X\)) generate this module at \(x\), hence their images in \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{Y/S}\) generate the latter at \(x\), one can find \(g_i\) (\(p+1 \leqslant i \leqslant n\)) in \(\mathscr{O}_X\) such that the \(dg'_i\) form a basis of \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{Y/S}\), then (by virtue of the exactness of (4.3)) complete the system of the \(dg_i\) (\(p+1 \leqslant i \leqslant n\)) into a basis of the middle term by elements of the form \(dg_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant n\)) where the \(g_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant p\)) are in \(\mathscr{J}_x\). The \(g_i\) come from sections of \(\mathscr{O}_X\) on a neighbourhood of \(x\) in \(X\), which one may suppose equal to \(X\). One is then under the conditions of 8 (ii), and we have established that this implies condition 8 (i), whence 10 (ii).

The implication (iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) in 10 follows at once from the implication (iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i) in 8. Hence (i), (ii), (iii) are equivalent, and imply (iv). Finally, it is trivial that (iv) implies (iii), taking into account that \(g_i \in \mathscr{J}_x\) which form a basis of \(\mathscr{J}_x \mod \mathscr{J}_x^2\) generate \(\mathscr{J}_x\) (Nakayama).
Moreover, the preceding proof shows the following:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 11

Let \(X\) be an \(S\)-prescheme, \(Y\) a closed sub-prescheme defined by a coherent sheaf \(\mathscr{J}\) of ideals on \(X\), \(x\) a point of \(Y\). Suppose \(X\) and \(Y\) smooth over \(S\) at \(x\). Let \(g_i\) be sections of \(\mathscr{J}\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant p\)). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    The \(g_i\) generate \(\mathscr{J}_x\) and the \(dg_i(x)\) are linearly independent in \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}(x)\) over \(\mathit{k}(x)\).

  2. (ii)

    The \(g_i \mod \mathscr{J}^2\) form a basis of \(\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2\) at \(x\).

  3. (iii)

    The \(g_i\) form a minimal system of generators of \(\mathscr{J}_x\).

  4. (iv)

    One can find other sections \(g_i\) (\(p+1 \leqslant i \leqslant n\)) of \(\mathscr{O}_X\) on a neighbourhood \(X_1\) of \(X\), defining together with the preceding ones a morphism étale \(X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'=S[t_1,\dots,t_n]\) such that \(Y_1=Y \cap X_1\) is the inverse image by \(g\) of the closed sub-prescheme \(Y'=S[t_{p+1},\dots,t_n]\) of \(X'=S[t_{1},\dots,t_n]\).
In particular:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 12

Let \(X\) be an \(S\)-prescheme, \(F\) a section of \(\mathscr{O}_X\), \(Y\) the sub-prescheme of zeros of \(F\) (defined by the coherent Ideal \(F\cdot\mathscr{O}_X\)), \(x\) a point of \(Y\). Suppose \(X\) simple over \(S\) at \(x\). For \(Y\) to be smooth over \(S\) at \(x\), it is necessary and sufficient that either \(F\) be zero in a neighbourhood of \(x\), or that \(dF(x) \neq 0\) (where \(dF(x)\) denotes the image of \(dF\) in the vector space \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/S}(x)\) over \(\mathit{k}(x)\)).

It is sufficient by virtue of 10 criterion (iii). It is necessary, for since \(\mathscr{J}\) is generated by a single element, it is first of all necessary that \(\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2\) at the point \(x\) be free of rank \(\leqslant 1\). If this rank is \(0\), i.e. \(\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2=0\) at \(x\), it follows that \(\mathscr{J}=0\) at \(x\) by Nakayama, i.e. \(F\) is zero in a neighbourhood of \(x\). If this rank is \(1\), then \(F\) forms a minimal system of generators of \(\mathscr{J}\) at \(x\), and one concludes by (11), equivalence of (i) and (iii)).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 13

Let \(Y\) be an \(S\)-prescheme locally of finite type, \(S'\) an \(S\)-pre­scheme flat, \(Y'=Y \times_S S'\), \(x'\) a point of \(Y'\) and \(x\) its canonical image in \(Y\). In order that \(Y\) be smooth over \(S\) at \(X\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(Y'\) be smooth over \(S'\) at \(x'\). In particular, if \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is flat and surjective, \(Y\) is smooth over \(S\) if and only if \(Y'\) is smooth over \(S'\).

There is only sufficiency to prove (necessity has been seen in 3 (ii)). One may suppose (replacing \(Y\) by a suitable neighbourhood of \(x\), \(Y'\) by the inverse image of the latter) that \(Y\) is affine of finite type over \(S\) affine, hence \(Y\) is isomorphic to a closed subprescheme of a scheme \(S[t_1,\dots,t_n]\). Consequently, \(Y'\) is identified with a closed subprescheme of \(X'=X \times_S S'\). Since \(X\) is smooth over \(S\), hence \(X'\) smooth over \(S'\), one may apply the smoothness criteria 10. Here, criterion (iv) gives the result at once.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 14

Criterion (iii) of Theorem 10 deserves to be called the Jacobian criterion for smoothness. It allows one to recognize, theoretically, whether a given \(S\)-prescheme \(Y\) is smooth over \(S\) at a point \(x\) of \(Y\), since there always exists a neighbourhood of \(Y\) isomorphic to a sub-prescheme of a smooth \(S\)-prescheme \(X\), for example \(X=S[t_1,\dots,t_n]\). It is moreover for \(X=S[t_1,\dots,t_n]\), \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), that one usually states the Jacobian criterion (of course, in the classical case considered by Zariski, \(A\) was a field). We leave it to the reader to give the statement relative to the data of an ideal \(J\) of \(A[t_1,\dots,t_n]\) and of a prime ideal containing it, to which one is thus led. Let us note that it does indeed seem at present (and especially since Nagata has succeeded in generalizing by non-differential methods Zariski's theorem stating that the set of regular points of an algebraic scheme is open) that the Jacobian criterion is hardly of any interest except in the form in which we give it here (i.e. using exclusively relative differentials and not absolute differentials, i.e. relative to the ring of absolute constants \(\mathbf{Z}\)). As is very often the case, the consideration of differentials is more convenient here than that of derivations. Let us note finally that if \(Y\) is smooth over \(S\) at \(x\), of relative dimension \(n\), then there exists an open neighbourhood of \(x\) in \(Y\) isomorphic to a sub-prescheme of \(X=S[t_1,\dots,t_n]\) with \(n=m+1\), as follows from the definition and from (I 6).

Let \(A\) be a Noetherian ring, \(x_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant n\)) elements of \(A\), \(J\) the ideal generated by the \(x_i\). One says that the \(x_i\) form a regular system of generators of \(J\) if the canonical surjective homomorphism

\[ (A/J)[t_1,\dots,t_n] \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{gr}^J(A) \]

defined by the \(x_i\) (where the second member denotes the graded ring associated with \(A\) filtered by the powers of \(J\)) is an isomorphism. This condition means also that

  1. (i)

    The canonical surjective homomorphism

    \[ S_{A/J} (J/J^2) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{gr}^J(A) \]

    (where the first member denotes the symmetric algebra of the \(A/J\)-module \(J/J^2\)) is an isomorphism, and

  2. (ii)

    \(J/J^2\) is free and admits as a basis the classes of the \(x_i \mod J^2\).

Under this form, one sees that if \(J\neq A\), the \(x_i\) form a minimal system of generators of \(J\), and that every other minimal system of generators of \(J\) is a regular system of generators (N.B. “minimal” is taken in the strict sense: minimum number of elements, which is equivalent to the sense: minimal for inclusion, only if \(A\) is local); on the other hand, if \(J=A\), every system of generators of \(J\) is regular.

The condition of regularity of a system of generators of an ideal is stable under localization by an arbitrary multiplicatively stable set, and on the other hand one sees immediately that in order for \((x_i)\) to be a minimal system of generators of \(J\), it already suffices that for every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\) containing \(J\), the \(x_i\) define a regular system of generators of \(JA_{\mathfrak{m}}\) in \(A_{\mathfrak{m}}\). This thus brings us back to the case where \(A\) is a local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\), and where the \(x_i\) are in \(\mathfrak{m}\). Then the \(x_i\) form a regular system of generators of \(J\) if and only if they form an \(A\)-sequence in the sense of Serre1, i.e. if for every \(i\) such that \(1 \leqslant i \leqslant n\), \(x_i\) is not a divisor of \(0\) in \(A/(x_1,\dots,x_{i-1})A\).

Finally, in the case where \(A\) is an algebra over a ring \(B\), and where \(A/J\) is isomorphic as a \(B\)-algebra to \(B\) (so that \(J\) is the kernel of a homomorphism of \(B\)-algebras \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\)), then the \(x_i\) form a regular system of generators of \(J\) if and only if the canonical homomorphism

\[ B[[ t_1,\dots,t_n]]\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widehat{A} \]

defined by the \(x_i\) (where the second member denotes the separated completion \(\varprojlim A/J^{n+1}\) of \(A\) for the topology defined by the powers of \(J\)) is an isomorphism (it is in any case surjective).

All these facts are well known, and no doubt appear in Serre's course on commutative algebra written up by Gabriel, to within very little. (Where one finds \(N\) other characterizations of \(A\)-sequences, in the case where \(A\) is a local ring).

Let \(J\) be an ideal in a Noetherian ring \(A\). One will say that \(J\) is a regular ideal if for every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(A\), \(JA_{\mathfrak{p}}\) admits a regular system of generators. It evidently suffices to verify this for \(\mathfrak{p} \supset J\), and one can moreover restrict to \(\mathfrak{p}\) maximal. More generally, let \(\mathscr{J}\) be an Ideal on a locally Noetherian prescheme \(X\); one says that \(\mathscr{J}\) is a regular Ideal if for every \(x\in X\), \(\mathscr{J}_x\) is an ideal of \(\mathscr{O}_x\) which admits a regular system of generators. This is equivalent to the conjunction of the two following conditions:

  1. (a)

    The canonical surjective homomorphism

    \[ S_{\mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{J}}(\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{gr}^{\mathscr{J}}(\mathscr{O}_X) \]

    is an isomorphism and

  2. (b)

    The sheaf of \(\mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{J}\)-Modules \(\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2\) is locally free.

One then also says that the sub-prescheme \(Y\) of \(X\) defined by \(\mathscr{J}\) (hence such that \(\mathscr{O}_Y\) extended by \(0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{J}\)) is regularly immersed in \(X\), and one defines in the same way (in an obvious manner) the notion of morphism of regular immersion, (resp. regular at a point \(x\)), a morphism of immersion \(Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) identifying \(Y\) (resp. a suitable neighbourhood of \(x\)), with a closed sub-prescheme regularly immersed in an open of \(X\). (One must not say: regular sub-prescheme, for that would mean that the local rings of \(Y\) are regular). Finally, sections \(x_i\) of \(\mathscr{J}\) are called a regular system of generators if for every \(x\in X\), the corresponding elements of \(\mathscr{O}_x\) form a regular system of generators of \(\mathscr{J}_x\) (terminology compatible with that introduced for generators of an ideal of a ring). This also means that the canonical surjective homomorphism

\[ \mathscr{O}_Y[t_1,\dots,t_n] \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{gr}^{\mathscr{J}} (\mathscr{O}_X) \]

defined by the \(x_i\) is an isomorphism. If one knows in advance that the Ideal \(\mathscr{J}\) is regular, this also means, simply, that at every point \(x\) of \(Y\), the \(x_i\) define a basis of \(\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2\) over \(\mathscr{O}_{Y,x}\). (N.B. this condition is vacuous if \(Y\) is empty). Thus, in order for \(\mathscr{J}\) to admit a regular system of generators, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathscr{J}\) be regular, and that the \(\mathscr{O}_Y\)-Module \(\mathscr{J} /\mathscr{J}^2\) be globally free (and not merely locally free), i.e. that the canonical homomorphism \(S_{\mathscr{O}_Y}(\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{gr}^{\mathscr{J}} (\mathscr{O}_X)\) be surjective, and that the \(\mathscr{O}_Y\)-Module \(\mathscr{J}/\mathscr{J}^2\) be globally free.

An augmented ring is said to be regular if the ideal of the augmentation is regular. Thus, if \(A\) is a local ring, considered as augmented in its residue field \(k\), then \(A\) is a regular local ring if and only if it is a regular augmented ring.

(To tell the truth, it seems that it was pointless to begin by making the preliminary sorites for rings; there is an advantage in beginning with sheaves right away. If one wants something in the Noetherian case, it is the definition adopted here — a priori less strict than that by Serre's \(A\)-sequences — which seems preferable for the needs of the differential calculus. Of course, to do things properly, one would have to develop also at least a part of the theory of smooth morphisms in the non-Noetherian setting2, probably starting from the Jacobian criterion, so as to obtain if possible all the essential formal properties of the smooth morphisms and of the étale morphisms, i.e. smooth and quasi-finite ones; the converses alone appealing to Noetherian hypotheses).
After these long terminological preliminaries, a small theorem:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 15

Let \(X\) be an \(S\)-prescheme locally of finite type, \(Y\) a closed sub-prescheme of \(X\) defined by a coherent sheaf \(\mathscr{J}\) of ideals on \(X\), \(x\) a point of \(X\). We now suppose \(Y\) smooth over \(S\) at \(x\) (and nothing about \(X\)). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(X\) is smooth over \(S\) at \(x\)

  2. (ii)

    The immersion \(i\colon Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is regular at \(x\), i.e. \(\mathscr{J}_x\) is a regular ideal of \(\mathscr{O}_x\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 16

Suppose \(Y\) smooth over \(S\). For \(X\) to be smooth over \(S\) in a neighbourhood of \(Y\) (i.e. at the points of \(Y\)) it is necessary and sufficient that \(Y\) be regularly embedded in \(X\), i.e. that the immersion \(i \colon Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be regular.
Proof

(i) implies (ii). We apply 10 criterion (ii); since \(g\colon X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is flat, in order to show that the inverse image under \(g\) of the sub-prescheme \(Y'\) of \(X'\) is regularly embedded, we are reduced to proving that \(Y'=S[t_{p+1},\dots, t_n]\) is regularly embedded in \(S[t_1,\dots,t_n]\), which is trivial (the \(t_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant p\)) form a regular system of generators of the Ideal defining \(Y'\) in \(X'\)).

(ii) implies (i). Let \(g_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant p\)) be a regular system of generators of \(\mathscr{J}_x\) and let \(g_i\) (\(p+1 \leqslant i \leqslant n\)) be elements of \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) such that their images \(g'_i\) in \(\mathscr{O}_{Y,x}\) define an étale morphism
\[ Y_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'=S[t_{p+1},\dots, t_n] \]

of a neighbourhood \(Y_1\) of \(Y\) in \(Y'\). The \(g_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant n\)) arise from sections (with the same names) of \(\mathscr{O}_X\) on a neighbourhood \(X_1\) of \(x\), and we may suppose \(X_1=X\), \(Y_1=Y\). We thus obtain a morphism
\[ g\colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'=S[t_1,\dots,t_n] \]

and everything comes down to showing that this morphism is étale at \(x\). Taking \(X_1\) small enough, we may suppose that the \(g_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant p\)) form a regular system of generators of \(\mathscr{J}\) over all of \(X\). In particular, they generate \(\mathscr{J}\), hence the sub-prescheme \(Y\) of \(X\) is identified with the inverse image under \(g\) of the sub-prescheme \(Y'\) of \(X'\). Let \(x'=g(x)\); then the fibre of \(X' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) at \(x'\) is identical to the fibre of \(Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\) at \(x\), hence is étale over \(\mathit{k}(x')\), hence \(g\) is unramified at \(x\); it remains to prove that \(g\) is flat at \(x\). Now the graded ring associated to \(\mathscr{O}_{X',x'}\) filtered by the powers of \(\mathscr{J}'_{x}\) is free over \(\mathscr{O}_{Y',x'}\) in all degrees; on the other hand the graded ring associated to \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) filtered by the powers of \(\mathscr{J}_x=\mathscr{J}'_x\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) is isomorphic (under the canonical homomorphism) to the tensor product of the preceding one by \(\mathscr{O}_{Y,x}\) (since both rings are polynomial rings in \(n-p\) indeterminates, with ring of constants \(\mathscr{O}_{Y',x'}\), resp. \(\mathscr{O}_{Y,x}\)); finally over \(\mathscr{O}_{X',x'}/\mathscr{J}'_{x'}= \mathscr{O}_{Y',x'}\) \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}/\mathscr{J}_x =\mathscr{O}_{Y,x}\) is flat.

By a general criterion of flatness (valid for a local homomorphism of Noetherian local rings \(A' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\), \(A'\) being equipped with an ideal \(J' \neq A'\) such that the associated graded ring is free over \(A'/J'\) in every dimension) it follows that \(X\) is flat over \(X'\) at \(x\), q.e.d.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 17

Let \(X\) be a prescheme locally of finite type over \(Y\), \(i\) a section of \(X\) over \(Y\), \(y\) a point of \(Y\), \(x=i(y)\), \(\mathscr{J}\) the sheaf of ideals on \(X\) defined by the sub-prescheme \(i(Y)\) (which we suppose closed in order to simplify the statement, a condition satisfied if \(X\) is a scheme).

The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(X\) is smooth over \(Y\) at \(x\)

  2. (ii)

    \(i\) is a regular immersion at \(y\)

  3. (iii)

    The completed \(\mathscr{O}_y\)-algebra of \(\mathscr{O}_x\) for the topology defined by the powers of \(\mathscr{J}_x\) is isomorphic to an algebra of formal power series \(\mathscr{O}_y[[ t_1,\dots,t_n]]\).

  4. (iii bis)

    There exists an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(y\) such that the sheaf of algebras \(\varprojlim i^*(\mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{J}^{n+1})\) over \(\mathscr{O}_Y\) is isomorphic to a sheaf of the form \(\mathscr{O}_Y[[ t_1,\dots, t_n]]\) over \(U\).

  5. (iv)

    There exists an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(y\), and an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(x\), and finally a \(Y\)-morphism \(g \colon V \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U[t_1,\dots, t_n]\), such that \(g\) is étale, that \(i\) induces a section of \(V\) over \(U\), transformed by \(g\) into the zero section of \(U[t_1,\dots, t_n]\) over \(U\).

The equivalence of (i) and (ii) is a particular case of theorem 15, taking \(Y=S\); the equivalence of (ii) and (iii) (and morally of (ii) and (iii bis)) was pointed out with the “reminders”. As for the equivalence of (i) and (iv), it is deduced easily from theorem 10 (equivalence of conditions (i) and (ii) thereof).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 18

Let \(X\) be a prescheme smooth over \(S\). Then the diagonal morphism

\[ \Delta_{X/S}\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_S X \]

is a regular immersion, or as one also says, \(X\) is “differentiably smooth” over \(S\).
Indeed, this is a particular case of corollary 16, since \(X\) and \(X\times_S X\) are both smooth over \(S\).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 18

Let us recall (I 1) that if \(X\) is a prescheme over \(S\), one introduces the quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras \(\mathscr{P}_{X/S}^n=\mathscr{O}_{X\times_S X}/\mathscr{I}_X^{n+1}\) on \(X\), (where \(\mathscr{I}_X\) denotes the sheaf of ideals which defines the diagonal in \(X\times_S X\)), considered as a sheaf of \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-algebras by means of the first projection \(\mathrm{pr}_1\colon X\times_S X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\). The \(\mathscr{P}_{X/S}^n\) form an inverse system of Algebras on \(X\), whose inverse limit is denoted \(\mathscr{P}_{X/S}^{\infty}\) and is none other than the structural sheaf of the formal completion of \(X\times_S X\) along the diagonal (now supposing \(X\) locally of finite type over \(S\), hence the \(\mathscr{P}_{X/S}^n\) coherent). To say that \(X\) is differentiably smooth over \(S\) (i.e. that the diagonal morphism \(\Delta_{X/S}\) is a regular immersion) also means that \(\mathscr{P}_{X/S}^{\infty}\) is regular, as a sheaf of algebras augmented towards \(\mathscr{O}_X\), i.e. that \(\mathit{\Omega}_{X/S}^1\) is locally free and the canonical surjective homomorphism

\[ S_{\mathscr{O}_X}(\mathit{\Omega}_{X/S}^1)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{gr}_*(\mathscr{P}_{X/S}^{\infty}) \]

is an isomorphism, or finally that every point of \(X\) has an open neighbourhood on which the sheaf of augmented algebras \(\mathscr{P}_{X/S}^{\infty}\) is isomorphic to a sheaf \(\mathscr{O}_X[[t_1,\dots,t_n]]\).

Let \(s\) be a section of \(X\) over \(S\), \(\mathscr{J}\) the sheaf of ideals on \(X\) which it defines (supposing for simplicity that \(s(S)\) is closed), one then has canonical isomorphisms of augmented \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-algebras:

\[ \tag{4.4} s^*(\mathscr{P}_{X/S}^n)=\mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{J}^{n+1},\quad s^*(\mathscr{P}_{X/S}^{\infty})= \varprojlim_{n} \mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{J}^{n+1} \]

These isomorphisms are functorial in an evident sense under change of base, and (taking account of this fact) give back a characterization of the sheaves of algebras \(\mathscr{P}_{X/S}^n\) on \(S\). If for example \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(k)\), \(k\) a field, then the datum of a section \(s\) of \(X\) over \(S\) is equivalent to the datum of a point \(x\) of \(X\) rational over \(k\), and the preceding formulas mean that one has an isomorphism of \(k\)-algebras

\[ \tag{4.5} \mathscr{P}_{X/S}^n(x)=\mathscr{O}_x/\mathfrak{m}_x^{n+1} \]

which justifies the name: “ sheaf of principal parts of order \(n\) on \(X\) rel. to \(S\) ” given to \(\mathscr{P}_{X/S}^n\). One sees moreover from (4.4) that if \(X\) is differentiably smooth over \(S\) at every point of \(s(S)\), then \(X\) is smooth over \(S\) at every point of \(s(S)\), (corollary 17) the converse being likewise true (corollary 18). Taking account of 13, one easily concludes from this that if \(X\) is an \(S\)-prescheme locally of finite type, \(X\) is smooth over \(S\) if and only if it is flat over \(S\) and differentiably smooth over \(S\). (N.B. the flatness hypothesis is essential, as one sees by taking for \(X\) a closed subprescheme of \(S\)).

Let us also note, by way of recollection, that one obtains a second algebra structure on \(\mathscr{P}_{X/S}^n\) by means of the projection \(\mathrm{pr}_2\colon X\times_S X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), which is moreover deduced from the preceding one by means of the canonical involution of the sheaf of rings \(\mathscr{P}_{X/S}^n\), induced by the symmetry automorphism of \(X\times_S X\). One denotes by \(d_{X/S}^n\) or simply \(d^n\), the homomorphism of sheaves of rings

\[ \tag{4.6} d_{X/S}^n\colon \mathscr{O}_X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{P}_{X/S}^n \]

which corresponds to this second Algebra structure. Taking account of the isomorphism (4.4), this homomorphism transforms a section \(f\) of \(\mathscr{O}_X\) into a section \(d^n(f)\) of \(\mathscr{P}_{X/S}^n\) whose inverse image under a section \(s\) of \(X\) over \(S\) is identified with the canonical image of \(f\) in \(\Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{J}^{n+1})\). This justifies the name of “ system of principal parts of order \(n\) of \(f\) ” given to \(d^nf\), notably in the case where \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(k)\), envisaged in formula (4.5).

To finish, let us note that the homomorphism (4.6) may be considered as the differential operator of order \(\leqslant n\)3 (relative to the prescheme of constants \(S\)) universal on \(\mathscr{O}_X\), agreeing to call a differential operator of order \(\leqslant n\) from \(\mathscr{O}_X\) into a Module \(F\), a homomorphism of sheaves \(D\) which factors as

\[ D\colon \mathscr{O}_X\xrightarrow{d^n} \mathscr{P}_{X/S}^n\xrightarrow{u} F \]

where \(u\) is a homomorphism of \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules, moreover uniquely determined by \(D\). This definition agrees with the intuitive recurrent definition (\(D\) is a differential operator of order \(\leqslant n\) if for every section \(g\) of \(\mathscr{O}_X\) on an open set \(U\) of \(X\), \(f\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} D(fg)-D(f)\) is a differential operator of order \(\leqslant n-1\) on \(U\)). It follows that if \(X\) is differentiably smooth over \(S\), the sheaf of rings of differential operators of all orders has the well-known simple structure in differential calculus on differentiable manifolds, and in particular admits locally a basis of \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module formed of the divided powers in permutable operators \(\delta/\delta x_i\) \((1\leqslant i\leqslant n)\). If \(S\) is a sheaf of \(\mathbf{Q}\)-algebras (\(\mathbf{Q}=\) field of rationals) it suffices to take the ordinary polynomials in the \(\delta/\delta x_i\). In this case moreover, and very exceptionally, for \(X\) to be differentiably smooth over \(S\), it already suffices that \(\mathit{\Omega}_{X/S}^1\) be locally free.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 19

The terminology “ regular immersion ”, “ regular ideal ”, etc. introduced in this article has met with a rather lively and general opposition (Chevalley, Serre). It has been proposed “ Cohen-Macaulay ideal ” or “ Macaulay ideal ” or “ Macaulayan ideal ”, which morally would oblige one to adopt also “ Cohen-Macaulay immersion ” or “ Macaulay immersion ”. This terminology however conflicts with another already employed in future redactions of the multiplodoque, where a morphism (of finite type) is said to be “ Cohen-Macaulay ” at a point if it is flat at this point, and if the fibre passing through this point has there a local ring which is a Cohen-Macaulay ring. Pending finding a satisfactory solution, we shall keep, with all reservations, the terminology introduced in this article4.

now rather say “\(A\)-regular sequence”, cf. EGA
\(0_{\textup{IV}}\) 15.1.7 and 15.1.11.

As
is said in the foreword, this is now done, cf. EGA

pages.title: The case of a base field
pages.html:
5 The case of a base field

Proposition 1

Let \(k\) be a field, \(X\) a prescheme of finite type over \(k\), \(x\) a point of \(X\) and \(n\) the dimension of \(X\) at \(x\), \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Spec} k[t_1,\dots,t_n]=Y\) a morphism, defined by elements \(f_i\in\Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X)\). The following conditions are equivalent (and imply that \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at \(x\), and a fortiori regular at \(x\) by 1):

  1. (i)

    \(f\) is étale at \(x\).

  2. (ii)

    The \(df_i\) form a basis of \(\mathit{\Omega}_{X/k}^1\) at \(x\).

  3. (iii)

    The \(df_i\) generate \(\mathit{\Omega}_{X/k}^1\) at \(x\).

Since (i) implies that \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at \(x\), the implication (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) is a particular case of 8, (ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii) is trivial, it remains to prove (iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i). Now if (iii) is satisfied, \(f\) is unramified at \(x\) by virtue of Lemma 1, hence (replacing \(x\) by an open neighbourhood) quasi-finite, hence dominant for reasons of dimension. Since \(Y\) is regular, it follows that \(f\) is étale by (I 5 (ii)) or (I 11).

Corollary 2

Under the preliminary conditions of 1, suppose that \(\mathit{k}(x)\) is a finite separable extension of \(k\), and that the \(f_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\)) define elements of \(\mathfrak{m}_x\). Then the preceding conditions are equivalent to

  1. (iv)

    The \(f_i\) form a system of generators of \(\mathfrak{m}_x\) (or again: the \(f_i\) mod \(\mathfrak{m}_x^2\) form a basis of \(\mathfrak{m}_x/\mathfrak{m}_x^2\) over \(\mathit{k}(x)\)).

Indeed, (iv)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii) by virtue of the exact sequence

\[ \tag{5.1} \mathfrak{m}_x/\mathfrak{m}_x^2\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\Omega_{\mathscr{O}_x/k}^1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Omega_{\mathit{k}(x)/k}^1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

and taking account of \(\Omega_{\mathit{k}(x)/k}^1=0\) since \(\mathit{k}(x)\) is étale over \(k\). On the other hand (ii) implies (iv), for since \(X\) and \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathit{k}(x))\) are smooth over \(k\) at \(x\), one can in the preceding exact sequence place a \(0\) on the left by virtue of 10 (iv).

Corollary 3

Let \(x\) be a point of \(X\) (of finite type over \(k\)). If \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at \(x\), then \(\mathscr{O}_x\) is regular, the converse being true if \(\mathit{k}(x)\) is a finite separable extension of \(k\).

Indeed, the converse follows from 2, by taking a regular system \((f_i)\) of generators of \(\mathfrak{m}_x\). (N.B. instead of 2, one may also invoke Theorem 15). We conclude:

Proposition 4

Let \(X\) be a prescheme of finite type over \(k\). If \(X\) is smooth over \(k\), it is regular, the converse being true if \(k\) is perfect.

For the converse, one notes that by virtue of 3, \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at every closed point, hence everywhere since the set of points where it is smooth is open.

Theorem 5

Let \(X\) be a prescheme of finite type over \(k\), \(x\) a point of \(X\), \(n\) the dimension of \(X\) at \(x\), \(k'\) a perfect extension of \(k\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at \(x\).

  2. (ii)

    \(\mathit{\Omega}_{X/k}^1\) is free of rank \(n\) at \(x\).

  3. (ii bis)

    \(\mathit{\Omega}_{X/k}^1\) is generated by \(n\) elements at \(x\).

  4. (iii)

    \(X\) is differentiably smooth over \(k\) at \(x\).

  5. (iv)

    There exists an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(x\) such that \(U\otimes_k k'\) is regular (i.e. the local rings of its points are regular).

We have (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) by 3 (ii), (ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii bis) trivially and (ii bis)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i) thanks to 1. Since \(X\) is flat over \(k\), we have (i)\(\Leftrightarrow\)(iii) by virtue of 18. We have (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iv) since smooth is invariant under extension of the base and implies regular, and (iv)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i) because by Proposition 4, we see that \(U\otimes_k k'\) is simple over \(k'\), hence \(U\) is simple over \(k\) by 13.

Taking for \(x\) the generic point of \(X\), assumed irreducible, we find:

Corollary 6

Let \(K\) be a local Artinian ring, a localization of an algebra of finite type over the field \(k\) (for example, \(K\) may be an extension of finite type of \(k\)), let \(n\) be the transcendence degree over \(K\) of its residue field. Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(K\) is a finite separable extension of a purely transcendental extension \(k(t_1,\dots,t_n)\) of \(k\).

  2. (ii)

    \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/k}\) is a free \(K\)-module of rank \(n\).

  3. (ii bis)

    \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/k}\) is a \(K\)-module admitting \(n\) generators.

  4. (iii)

    The completion \(O'\) of \(K\otimes_k K\) for the topology defined by the powers of the augmentation ideal \(K\otimes_k K\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K\) is a \(K\)-algebra augmented “regular”, i.e. isomorphic to an algebra of formal power series in \(K\) (N.B. If \(K\) is a field, this is equivalent to saying that \(O'\) is a regular local ring).

  5. (iv)

    \(K\) is a separable extension of \(k\).

Indeed, one can always regard \(K\) as the local ring of the generic point of an irreducible scheme of finite type \(X\) over \(k\), and the conditions considered are the conditions of the same name in 5, taking in (iv) for \(k'\) an algebraically closed extension of \(k\). Only the implication \(K\) separable over \(k\) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) \(X\) smooth over \(k\) at \(x\), requires a proof. Now one is at once reduced, thanks to 13, to the case where the base field is \(k'\), hence algebraically closed, hence where there exists a point \(a\) of \(X\) rational over \(k\). But then \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at \(a\) by 4, a fortiori it is smooth over \(k\) at the generic point \(x\), q.e.d.1.

One will note that in the case where \(K\) is an extension of finite type of \(k\), the equivalence of (i), (ii), (ii bis), (iv) is well known, but that we have not made use of any of these equivalences already known. Of course, the proposition 1 contains as a particular case that a sequence of elements \(x_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\)) is a “separating transcendence basis” of \(K\) over \(k\) if and only if the \(dx_i\) form a basis of the \(K\)-module \(\Omega^1_{K/k}\), a fact well known otherwise.

Corollary 7

Let \(X\) be a prescheme of finite type over a field \(k\). In order that \(X\) be smooth over \(k\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/k}\) be locally free, and that the local rings of the generic points of the irreducible components of \(X\) be separable extensions of \(k\) (this last condition being automatically satisfied if \(k\) is perfect and \(X\) reduced).

We may assume \(X\) connected; let \(n\) be the rank of \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/k}\) supposed locally free. By the hypothesis and 6, it is also the transcendence degree of the extensions of \(k\) defined by the local rings of the generic points of \(X\), so all the irreducible components of \(X\) are of dimension \(n\). One then concludes by means of 5.

One must be careful that if \(K\) is a finite extension (not necessarily separable) of \(k\), then \(\Omega^1_{K/k}\) is a free \(k\)-module, so setting \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(K)\), \(\Omega^1_{X/k}\) is a locally free sheaf, and \(X\) is reduced, without \(X\) being necessarily smooth over \(k\). Then extending the scalars to the algebraic closure of \(k\), one finds an analogous example, where \(k\) is algebraically closed, but \(X\) on the other hand not being reduced.

Corollary 8

Let \(X\) be a prescheme of finite type over the field \(k\), \(x\) a point of \(X\), \(n\) the dimension of \(X\) at \(x\), \(p\) the dimension of \(\mathscr{O}_x\), i.e. the codimension in \(X\) of the closure \(Y\) of \(x\) in \(X\); hence \(n-p\) the transcendence degree of \(\mathit{k}(x)\) over \(k\). Let \(f_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\)) be elements of \(\mathscr{O}_x\), such that \(f_i\in \mathfrak{m}_x\) for \(1\leqslant i\leqslant p\). The following conditions are equivalent

  1. (i)

    the germ of morphism at \(x\)

    \[ X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec}\bigl(k[t_1,\dots,t_n]\bigr) \]

    defined by the \(f_i\) is étale at \(x\).

  2. (ii)

    The \(f_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant p\)) generate \(\mathfrak{m}_x\), i.e. form a regular system of parameters of \(\mathscr{O}_x\), and the classes in \(\mathit{k}(x)\) of the \(f_j\) (\(p+1\leqslant j\leqslant n\)) form a separating transcendence basis, (i.e. the \(d\overline{f}_j\) (\(p+1\leqslant j\leqslant n\)) form a basis of \(\Omega^1_{\mathit{k}(x)/k}\), or again generate \(\Omega^1_{\mathit{k}(x)/k}\)).

Suppose (i) verified. It follows that the \(df_i(x)\) form a basis of \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/k}(x)\) (8) hence their images \(d\overline{f}_i(x)\) in \(\Omega^1_{\mathit{k}(x)/k}\) generate this vector space over \(k\). Since the \(\overline{f}_i\) for \(1\leqslant i\leqslant p\) are zero, it follows that it suffices to take the \(d\overline{f}_i(x)\) with \(p+1\leqslant i\leqslant n\). Since the transcendence degree of \(\mathit{k}(x)\) over \(k\) is \(n-p\), it then follows from corollary 6 criterion (iii) (applied to \(K=\mathit{k}(x)\)) that \(Y\) is smooth over \(k\) at its generic point \(x\), and that the \(d\overline{f}_i(x)\) (\(p+1\leqslant i\leqslant n\)) form a basis of \(\Omega^1_{\mathit{k}(x)/k}\) over \(\mathit{k}(x)\). Consequently, condition (ii) of 9 is verified, hence also condition (iii) and in particular the \(f_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant p\)) form a system of generators of \(\mathfrak{m}_x\). Since \(\mathscr{O}_x\) is of dimension \(p\), they thus form a regular system of parameters at \(x\). This proves (ii).

Suppose (ii) verified. By virtue of the exact sequence (1), it follows that the \(df_i(x)\) generate \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/k}\), whence (i) by means of prop. 1.

Corollary 9

Let \(X\) be a prescheme of finite type over the field \(k\), \(x\) a point of \(X\), \(n\) the dimension of \(X\) at \(x\), \(p\) the dimension of \(\mathscr{O}_x\), i.e. the codimension of the closure \(Y\) of \(x\) in \(X\), hence \(n-p\) the transcendence degree of \(\mathit{k}(x)\) over \(k\). Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(\mathscr{O}_x\) is regular and \(\mathit{k}(x)\) is a separable extension of \(k\).

  2. (ii)

    \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at \(x\), and the canonical homomorphism

    \[ \mathfrak{m}_x/\mathfrak{m}_x^2\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Omega^1_{\mathscr{O}_x/k}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_x} \mathit{k}(x)=\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/k}(x) \]

    is injective.

  3. (iii)

    There exist \(f_i\in \mathscr{O}_x\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\)) with \(f_i\in \mathfrak{m}_x\) for \(1\leqslant i\leqslant p\), such that the germ of morphism at \(x\) of \(X\) into \(\operatorname{Spec}\bigl(k[t_1,\dots,t_n]\bigr)\) defined by the \(f_i\) is étale at \(x\) (i.e. by 1 such that the \(df_i(x)\) generate \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/k}(x)\)).

  4. (iv)

    There exist \(f_i\in \mathscr{O}_x\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\)) such that the \(f_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant p\)) generate \(\mathfrak{m}_x\) and the \(df_j(x)\) (\(p+1\leqslant j\leqslant n\)) generate \(\Omega^1_{\mathit{k}(x)/k}\) over \(\mathit{k}(x)\).

The equivalence of (iii) and (iv) follows from corollary 8; these conditions are also equivalent, by 9, to the fact that \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at \(x\) and that condition (ii) of 10 is verified. Hence they are equivalent to the fact that \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at \(x\) and that condition (iv) of 10 is verified, hence to 9 (ii). Or to the fact that \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at \(x\) and that condition (i) of 10 is verified, which here means simply that \(\mathit{k}(x)\) is separable over \(k\). This implies 9 (i); it remains to prove that 9 (i) implies it, i.e. to prove the

Corollary 10

Let \(x\) be a point of a prescheme of finite type over the field \(k\), such that \(\mathit{k}(x)\) is separable over \(k\). For \(X\) to be smooth over \(k\) at \(x\), it is necessary and sufficient that it be regular at \(x\) (i.e. that the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_x\) be regular).

Indeed, if this is so, one can evidently find \(f_i\in \mathscr{O}_x\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\)) satisfying condition 9 (iv).

5 Errata

In the present article, proof of 6, one has used the fact that a nonempty reduced scheme of finite type over an algebraically closed field admits at least one regular point (hence smooth), a fact which is usually proved by differential means (via Zariski's theorem that the set of regular points of \(X\) is open). If one wishes to avoid a vicious circle, one must prove that if \(K/k\) is a separable extension of finite type, and if the \(f_i\in K\) are such that \(d_{K/k}f_i\) form a basis of \(\Omega^1_{K/k}\), (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\)), then \(n\) is the transcendence degree of \(K\) over \(k\), i.e. the \(f_i\) are algebraically independent. The proof of this fact by means of Mac Lane's criterion is well known, cf. Bourbaki, Algèbre, Chap. V par. 9 th. 2: one takes a polynomial \(g\in k[t_1,\dots,t_n]\) of minimal degree such that \(g(f_1,\dots,f_n)=0\), one thus has

\[ \sum\frac{dg}{d t_i}(f_1,\dots,f_n) df_i=0 \]

whence (since the \(df_i\) form a basis of \(\Omega^1_{K/k}\)) the fact that the \(dg/d t_i\) annihilate \((f_1,\dots,f_n)\), hence are zero by the minimal character of \(g\), so that if \(k\) is of characteristic \(0\) one has \(g=0\), and if \(k\) is of characteristic \(p\neq 0\) one has \(g=h(t_1^p,\dots,t_n^p)\). Using Mac Lane's criterion, one sees that the polynomial \(h\in k[t_1,\dots,t_n]\) also annihilates \((f_1,\dots,f_n)\), whence again \(g=0\) by the minimal character of \(g\).

blocks.html: 5 The case of a base field

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1

Let \(k\) be a field, \(X\) a prescheme of finite type over \(k\), \(x\) a point of \(X\) and \(n\) the dimension of \(X\) at \(x\), \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Spec} k[t_1,\dots,t_n]=Y\) a morphism, defined by elements \(f_i\in\Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X)\). The following conditions are equivalent (and imply that \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at \(x\), and a fortiori regular at \(x\) by 1):

  1. (i)

    \(f\) is étale at \(x\).

  2. (ii)

    The \(df_i\) form a basis of \(\mathit{\Omega}_{X/k}^1\) at \(x\).

  3. (iii)

    The \(df_i\) generate \(\mathit{\Omega}_{X/k}^1\) at \(x\).

Since (i) implies that \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at \(x\), the implication (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) is a particular case of 8, (ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii) is trivial, it remains to prove (iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i). Now if (iii) is satisfied, \(f\) is unramified at \(x\) by virtue of Lemma 1, hence (replacing \(x\) by an open neighbourhood) quasi-finite, hence dominant for reasons of dimension. Since \(Y\) is regular, it follows that \(f\) is étale by (I 5 (ii)) or (I 11).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

Under the preliminary conditions of 1, suppose that \(\mathit{k}(x)\) is a finite separable extension of \(k\), and that the \(f_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\)) define elements of \(\mathfrak{m}_x\). Then the preceding conditions are equivalent to

  1. (iv)

    The \(f_i\) form a system of generators of \(\mathfrak{m}_x\) (or again: the \(f_i\) mod \(\mathfrak{m}_x^2\) form a basis of \(\mathfrak{m}_x/\mathfrak{m}_x^2\) over \(\mathit{k}(x)\)).
Indeed, (iv)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii) by virtue of the exact sequence

\[ \tag{5.1} \mathfrak{m}_x/\mathfrak{m}_x^2\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\Omega_{\mathscr{O}_x/k}^1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Omega_{\mathit{k}(x)/k}^1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

and taking account of \(\Omega_{\mathit{k}(x)/k}^1=0\) since \(\mathit{k}(x)\) is étale over \(k\). On the other hand (ii) implies (iv), for since \(X\) and \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathit{k}(x))\) are smooth over \(k\) at \(x\), one can in the preceding exact sequence place a \(0\) on the left by virtue of 10 (iv).

blocks.html:
Corollary 3

Let \(x\) be a point of \(X\) (of finite type over \(k\)). If \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at \(x\), then \(\mathscr{O}_x\) is regular, the converse being true if \(\mathit{k}(x)\) is a finite separable extension of \(k\).
Indeed, the converse follows from 2, by taking a regular system \((f_i)\) of generators of \(\mathfrak{m}_x\). (N.B. instead of 2, one may also invoke Theorem 15). We conclude:

blocks.html:
Proposition 4

Let \(X\) be a prescheme of finite type over \(k\). If \(X\) is smooth over \(k\), it is regular, the converse being true if \(k\) is perfect.
For the converse, one notes that by virtue of 3, \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at every closed point, hence everywhere since the set of points where it is smooth is open.

Theorem 5

Let \(X\) be a prescheme of finite type over \(k\), \(x\) a point of \(X\), \(n\) the dimension of \(X\) at \(x\), \(k'\) a perfect extension of \(k\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at \(x\).

  2. (ii)

    \(\mathit{\Omega}_{X/k}^1\) is free of rank \(n\) at \(x\).

    \(\mathit{\Omega}_{X/k}^1\) is generated by \(n\) elements at \(x\).

  4. (iii)

    \(X\) is differentiably smooth over \(k\) at \(x\).

  5. (iv)

    There exists an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(x\) such that \(U\otimes_k k'\) is regular (i.e. the local rings of its points are regular).

We have (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) by 3 (ii), (ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii bis) trivially and (ii bis)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i) thanks to 1. Since \(X\) is flat over \(k\), we have (i)\(\Leftrightarrow\)(iii) by virtue of 18. We have (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iv) since smooth is invariant under extension of the base and implies regular, and (iv)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i) because by Proposition 4, we see that \(U\otimes_k k'\) is simple over \(k'\), hence \(U\) is simple over \(k\) by 13.
Taking for \(x\) the generic point of \(X\), assumed irreducible, we find:

blocks.html:
Corollary 6

Let \(K\) be a local Artinian ring, a localization of an algebra of finite type over the field \(k\) (for example, \(K\) may be an extension of finite type of \(k\)), let \(n\) be the transcendence degree over \(K\) of its residue field. Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(K\) is a finite separable extension of a purely transcendental extension \(k(t_1,\dots,t_n)\) of \(k\).

  2. (ii)

    \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/k}\) is a free \(K\)-module of rank \(n\).

  3. (ii bis)

    \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/k}\) is a \(K\)-module admitting \(n\) generators.

  4. (iii)

    The completion \(O'\) of \(K\otimes_k K\) for the topology defined by the powers of the augmentation ideal \(K\otimes_k K\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K\) is a \(K\)-algebra augmented “regular”, i.e. isomorphic to an algebra of formal power series in \(K\) (N.B. If \(K\) is a field, this is equivalent to saying that \(O'\) is a regular local ring).

  5. (iv)

    \(K\) is a separable extension of \(k\).

Indeed, one can always regard \(K\) as the local ring of the generic point of an irreducible scheme of finite type \(X\) over \(k\), and the conditions considered are the conditions of the same name in 5, taking in (iv) for \(k'\) an algebraically closed extension of \(k\). Only the implication \(K\) separable over \(k\) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) \(X\) smooth over \(k\) at \(x\), requires a proof. Now one is at once reduced, thanks to 13, to the case where the base field is \(k'\), hence algebraically closed, hence where there exists a point \(a\) of \(X\) rational over \(k\). But then \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at \(a\) by 4, a fortiori it is smooth over \(k\) at the generic point \(x\), q.e.d.1.

One will note that in the case where \(K\) is an extension of finite type of \(k\), the equivalence of (i), (ii), (ii bis), (iv) is well known, but that we have not made use of any of these equivalences already known. blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7

Let \(X\) be a prescheme of finite type over a field \(k\). In order that \(X\) be smooth over \(k\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/k}\) be locally free, and that the local rings of the generic points of the irreducible components of \(X\) be separable extensions of \(k\) (this last condition being automatically satisfied if \(k\) is perfect and \(X\) reduced).

We may assume \(X\) connected; let \(n\) be the rank of \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/k}\) supposed locally free. By the hypothesis and 6, it is also the transcendence degree of the extensions of \(k\) defined by the local rings of the generic points of \(X\), so all the irreducible components of \(X\) are of dimension \(n\). One then concludes by means of 5.

One must be careful that if \(K\) is a finite extension (not necessarily separable) of \(k\), then \(\Omega^1_{K/k}\) is a free \(k\)-module, so setting \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(K)\), \(\Omega^1_{X/k}\) is a locally free sheaf, and \(X\) is reduced, without \(X\) being necessarily smooth over \(k\). Then extending the scalars to the algebraic closure of \(k\), one finds an analogous example, where \(k\) is algebraically closed, but \(X\) on the other hand not being reduced.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8

Let \(X\) be a prescheme of finite type over the field \(k\), \(x\) a point of \(X\), \(n\) the dimension of \(X\) at \(x\), \(p\) the dimension of \(\mathscr{O}_x\), i.e. the codimension in \(X\) of the closure \(Y\) of \(x\) in \(X\); hence \(n-p\) the transcendence degree of \(\mathit{k}(x)\) over \(k\). Let \(f_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\)) be elements of \(\mathscr{O}_x\), such that \(f_i\in \mathfrak{m}_x\) for \(1\leqslant i\leqslant p\). The following conditions are equivalent

  1. (i)

    the germ of morphism at \(x\)

    \[ X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec}\bigl(k[t_1,\dots,t_n]\bigr) \]

    defined by the \(f_i\) is étale at \(x\).

  2. (ii)

    The \(f_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant p\)) generate \(\mathfrak{m}_x\), i.e. form a regular system of parameters of \(\mathscr{O}_x\), and the classes in \(\mathit{k}(x)\) of the \(f_j\) (\(p+1\leqslant j\leqslant n\)) form a separating transcendence basis, (i.e. the \(d\overline{f}_j\) (\(p+1\leqslant j\leqslant n\)) form a basis of \(\Omega^1_{\mathit{k}(x)/k}\), or again generate \(\Omega^1_{\mathit{k}(x)/k}\)).

Suppose (i) verified. It follows that the \(df_i(x)\) form a basis of \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/k}(x)\) (8) hence their images \(d\overline{f}_i(x)\) in \(\Omega^1_{\mathit{k}(x)/k}\) generate this vector space over \(k\). Since the \(\overline{f}_i\) for \(1\leqslant i\leqslant p\) are zero, it follows that it suffices to take the \(d\overline{f}_i(x)\) with \(p+1\leqslant i\leqslant n\). Since the transcendence degree of \(\mathit{k}(x)\) over \(k\) is \(n-p\), it then follows from corollary 6 criterion (iii) (applied to \(K=\mathit{k}(x)\)) that \(Y\) is smooth over \(k\) at its generic point \(x\), and that the \(d\overline{f}_i(x)\) (\(p+1\leqslant i\leqslant n\)) form a basis of \(\Omega^1_{\mathit{k}(x)/k}\) over \(\mathit{k}(x)\). Consequently, condition (ii) of 9 is verified, hence also condition (iii) and in particular the \(f_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant p\)) form a system of generators of \(\mathfrak{m}_x\). Since \(\mathscr{O}_x\) is of dimension \(p\), they thus form a regular system of parameters at \(x\). This proves (ii).
Suppose (ii) verified. By virtue of the exact sequence (1), it follows that the \(df_i(x)\) generate \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/k}\), whence (i) by means of prop. 1.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9

Let \(X\) be a prescheme of finite type over the field \(k\), \(x\) a point of \(X\), \(n\) the dimension of \(X\) at \(x\), \(p\) the dimension of \(\mathscr{O}_x\), i.e. the codimension of the closure \(Y\) of \(x\) in \(X\), hence \(n-p\) the transcendence degree of \(\mathit{k}(x)\) over \(k\). Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(\mathscr{O}_x\) is regular and \(\mathit{k}(x)\) is a separable extension of \(k\).

  2. (ii)

    \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at \(x\), and the canonical homomorphism

    \[ \mathfrak{m}_x/\mathfrak{m}_x^2\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Omega^1_{\mathscr{O}_x/k}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_x} \mathit{k}(x)=\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/k}(x) \]

    is injective.

  3. (iii)

    There exist \(f_i\in \mathscr{O}_x\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\)) with \(f_i\in \mathfrak{m}_x\) for \(1\leqslant i\leqslant p\), such that the germ of morphism at \(x\) of \(X\) into \(\operatorname{Spec}\bigl(k[t_1,\dots,t_n]\bigr)\) defined by the \(f_i\) is étale at \(x\) (i.e. by 1 such that the \(df_i(x)\) generate \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/k}(x)\)).

  4. (iv)

    There exist \(f_i\in \mathscr{O}_x\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\)) such that the \(f_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant p\)) generate \(\mathfrak{m}_x\) and the \(df_j(x)\) (\(p+1\leqslant j\leqslant n\)) generate \(\Omega^1_{\mathit{k}(x)/k}\) over \(\mathit{k}(x)\).

The equivalence of (iii) and (iv) follows from corollary 8; these conditions are also equivalent, by 9, to the fact that \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at \(x\) and that condition (ii) of 10 is verified. Hence they are equivalent to the fact that \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at \(x\) and that condition (iv) of 10 is verified, hence to 9 (ii). Or to the fact that \(X\) is smooth over \(k\) at \(x\) and that condition (i) of 10 is verified, which here means simply that \(\mathit{k}(x)\) is separable over \(k\). This implies 9 (i); it remains to prove that 9 (i) implies it, i.e. to prove the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 10

Let \(x\) be a point of a prescheme of finite type over the field \(k\), such that \(\mathit{k}(x)\) is separable over \(k\). For \(X\) to be smooth over \(k\) at \(x\), it is necessary and sufficient that it be regular at \(x\) (i.e. that the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_x\) be regular).
Indeed, if this is so, one can evidently find \(f_i\in \mathscr{O}_x\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\)) satisfying condition 9 (iv).
5 Errata

In the present article, proof of 6, one has used the fact that a nonempty reduced scheme of finite type over an algebraically closed field admits at least one regular point (hence smooth), a fact which is usually proved by differential means (via Zariski's theorem that the set of regular points of \(X\) is open). If one wishes to avoid a vicious circle, one must prove that if \(K/k\) is a separable extension of finite type, and if the \(f_i\in K\) are such that \(d_{K/k}f_i\) form a basis of \(\Omega^1_{K/k}\), (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\)), then \(n\) is the transcendence degree of \(K\) over \(k\), i.e. the \(f_i\) are algebraically independent. The proof of this fact by means of Mac Lane's criterion is well known, cf. Bourbaki, Algèbre, Chap. V par. 9 th. 2: one takes a polynomial \(g\in k[t_1,\dots,t_n]\) of minimal degree such that \(g(f_1,\dots,f_n)=0\), one thus has
\[ \sum\frac{dg}{d t_i}(f_1,\dots,f_n) df_i=0 \]

whence (since the \(df_i\) form a basis of \(\Omega^1_{K/k}\)) the fact that the \(dg/d t_i\) annihilate \((f_1,\dots,f_n)\), hence are zero by the minimal character of \(g\), so that if \(k\) is of characteristic \(0\) one has \(g=0\), and if \(k\) is of characteristic \(p\neq 0\) one has \(g=h(t_1^p,\dots,t_n^p)\). Using Mac Lane's criterion, one sees that the polynomial \(h\in k[t_1,\dots,t_n]\) also annihilates \((f_1,\dots,f_n)\), whence again \(g=0\) by the minimal character of \(g\).
